claims.claim: Neglecting natural convection in the model resulted in a maximum peak temperature difference of -150 K and a maximum time-to-peak difference of 52.0 s compared to the full proposed model.
claims.quote: the maximum differences in the peak temperature between the proposed model and the model without convection were (cid:0) 150 K and 52.0 s, respectively

claims.claim: Neglecting thermal radiation in the model resulted in a maximum peak temperature difference of -52.4 K and a maximum time-to-peak difference of -125.0 s compared to the full proposed model.
claims.quote: the differences between the proposed model and the model without radiation were (cid:0) 52.4 K and (cid:0) 125.0 s, respectively

claims.claim: Increasing the cell-to-cell gap accelerated heat transfer but compromised energy density for the battery pack.
claims.quote: the presence of a cell-to-cell gap accelerated heat transfer but compromised energy density for the battery pack

claims.claim: When the cell-to-cell gap is 0 mm, natural convection does not change the sequence in which cells enter thermal runaway because there is no airflow.
claims.quote: natural convection did not change the sequence of the first and last cells entering thermal runaway. This may have been because there was no airflow, as the cell-to-cell gap was 0 mm in these simulations.

claims.claim: The larger the cell-to-cell gap, the larger the time interval until the last cell starts thermal runaway, due to increased heat dissipation facilitated by the broader gap.
claims.quote: the larger the gap, the larger the time interval until the last cell starts thermal runaway. This may be due to the increase in clearance and faster heat dissipation facilitated by the broader gap.

claims.claim: In a 20×20 battery pack with d=0 mm, α=90°, SOC=100%, and Tamb=25°C, approximately 86 seconds after the first thermal runaway reaction, all batteries in the pack were triggered.
claims.quote: Approximately 86 s after the occurrence of the ﬁrst runaway reaction, the TRs of all the batteries are triggered.

claims.claim: The ISC triggering condition in the model is set as when the jellyroll node temperature reaches the separator melting point of 165°C for the chosen NCA 18650 battery.
claims.quote: The ISC triggering condition is set as when the temperature of the jellyroll node reaches the separator melting point [39, 40] (165 °C for the chosen battery).

claims.claim: Under Mode I (low thermal contact resistance θc), local overheating on the battery surface causes the surface temperature to exceed the separator melting point quickly, producing a local short circuit that further accelerates thermal runaway triggering.
claims.quote: Mode I: low θc leads to a local overheating in the battery surface. The surface temperature exceeds the melting point of the separator quickly. The melting of the separator produces a local short circuit. This early-triggered ISC further accelerates TR triggering.

claims.claim: Under Mode II (high thermal contact resistance θc), conductive heat cannot concentrate on the battery surface, the surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time, and total TR propagation time is significantly longer than in Mode I.
claims.quote: Mode II: high θc prevents conductive heat from concentrating on the battery surface. The surface and jellyroll average temperatures converge over time. When the temperatures reach the TTR (melting point of the separator), the TR will inevitably be triggered. The total time of TR propagation is signiﬁcantly longer than that of Mode I.

claims.claim: The simulation indicates a threshold thermal contact resistance of θc = 0.89 K/W separating Mode I and Mode II thermal runaway propagation behavior.
claims.quote: The simulation indicates that the threshold θc = 0.89 K/W.

claims.claim: TR propagation speed increases with increasing ambient temperature and increases when battery spacing decreases, because smaller spacing reduces equivalent thermal resistance and accelerates heat transfer.
claims.quote: First, the TR propagation speed increases with Tamb, because a high Tamb corresponds to a high initial thermal energy within each battery. Second, the TR propagation speed increases when battery spacing d decreases. Decreasing d means decreasing the equivalent thermal resistance. The lower the thermal resistance, the quicker the heat transfer.

claims.claim: Battery packs with random heat transfer coefficients between 0 W/K and 2.7 W/K (average 1.35 W/K) showed TR propagation that was stable but slightly slower than packs with uniform thermal coefficients.
claims.quote: Simulation results show that the TR propagations of the battery packs with random thermal coeﬃcients are stable and little slower than the battery packs with even thermal coeﬃcients.

claims.claim: For a two-battery pack at d=0 mm (Mode I), the total TR propagation time Ttotal is approximately 3 seconds, whereas at d=0.01 mm (Mode II), Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 seconds.
claims.quote: Normally, the Ttotal of a two-battery pack at d = 0 mm is approximately 3 s (Fig. 6a, mode I). For another case at d = 0.01 mm, the Ttotal is prolonged to 18.2 s (Fig. 6b, mode II).

claims.claim: For the pouch cell (Cell A), T3 detected inside the cell was 925.0 °C, T3 detected between two cells was 908.5 °C, and T3 at the cell surface was only 508.3 °C during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the pouch cell, the maximum temperature T3 detected inside the cell was similar to that detected between two cells, at 925.0 °C and 908.5 °C, respectively, versus only 508.3 °C at the cell surface.

claims.claim: For the prismatic cell (Cell B), T3 detected inside the cell was 959.0 °C, compared to 806.0 °C between two cells, and only 381.4 °C at the cell surface during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the prismatic cell, T3 detected inside the cell was 959.0 °C, compared to 806.0 °C between two cells, and only 381.4 °C at the cell surface.

claims.claim: For the cylindrical cell (Cell C), T3 detected inside the cell was 1222.2 °C, which is hundreds of degrees higher than the 600.4 °C detected at the cell surface during EV-ARC thermal runaway testing.
claims.quote: For the cylindrical cell, T3 detected inside the cell was 1222.2 °C, which is hundreds of degrees higher than that detected by the cell surface.

claims.claim: From onset temperature T1 to triggering temperature T2, surface temperature measurements are sufficient for investigating adiabatic side reactions because the heat generation rate is low enough to allow temperature equilibration between the cell interior and exterior.
claims.quote: Because the heat generation rate was low at this stage, the temperature could reach equilibrium, and therefore, the T1 values were the same whether they were detected inside or outside the cell.

claims.claim: After the triggering temperature T2, the internal temperature detection is the most accurate method for evaluating the maximum temperature and rate of temperature increase, because heat production increases faster near T2 and heat cannot transfer quickly from the cell interior to the surface.
claims.quote: However, heat production increases faster in the vicinity of T2, and a delay in the temperature responses can explain the phenomena observed in Fig. 9(a) and (b), where the internal temperature can be seen to reach T2 and increased sharply. However, the temperature detected between two cells increased tilt.

claims.claim: The maximum dT/dt detected by the internal thermocouple of the prismatic cell (Cell B) was 24.2 times higher than that detected between two cells, and 11.2 times higher than that measured at the cell surface during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum dT·dt−1 detected inside Cell B was 24.2 times higher than that detected between two cells, and 11.2 times higher than that measured at the cell surface.

claims.claim: The simulated internal temperature method (thermocouple between two identical cells) is an appropriate substitute for internal temperature detection only for the pouch cell, but is inaccurate for prismatic and cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: The placement of thermocouple between two identical cells is an appropriate substitute for the internal temperature detection method only for the pouch cell. For prismatic and cylindrical cells, the most accurate method is to directly measure the temperature inside the cell.

claims.claim: Energy release of the cell body during thermal runaway will be largely underestimated if evaluated from surface temperature measurements, with the internal measurement yielding approximately twice the energy of surface measurements for the pouch cell.
claims.quote: the energy calculated from the internal measurements was twice that calculated from surface measurements.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway behavior of a cell does not change significantly when capacity fade is less than 10% by different aging paths.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway behavior of cell does not have a significant change when the capacity fade is less than 10%, by different aging paths [24].

claims.claim: The cell with a higher nickel proportion in the cathode material always has less thermal stability, which is reflected by a lower T1 onset temperature.
claims.quote: The T1 value of Cell C was lower than that of Cell A and Cell B, led by the unstable of cathode material. The cell with more nickel proportion in the cathode material always has less thermal stability.
